1:
"I commend unto you Phebe our sister, which is a servant of the church which is at Cenchrea:" The witnesses note the commendation of Phebe, a servant of the church. Gill notes this chapter consists chiefly of commendations and salutations, and begins with the former; it was usual to give letters of commendation for a member of one church to another; Phebe, by the subscription of the epistle, was the bearer of this letter. The witnesses note Paul commending Phebe, a sister and a servant (deaconess) of the church at Cenchrea; the letter of commendation for the faithful sister who carried the epistle, and the notice of her service to the church — the office or work of ministering, the honour given to a faithful woman in the early church; the commendation of the servant. The witnesses note Phebe our sister. The witnesses note a servant of the church.

The witnesses note the commendation of Phebe, a servant of the church at Cenchrea — the honour given to a faithful woman, the bearer of the letter.

So the witnesses make the verse the commendation of Phebe. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour given to a faithful woman who served the church, and the practice of letters of commendation. The witnesses note the commendation: "I commend unto you Phebe our sister" — Gill notes it was usual to give letters of commendation for a member of one church to another; Paul commends Phebe; the commendation; the introduction; the letter of recommendation; the sister vouched for. The expositors observe the practice: such letters of commendation introduced and vouched for travelling believers; the practice; the commending letters; the fellowship between churches; the believer recommended; the early custom. The witnesses press the service of Phebe: "a servant of the church which is at Cenchrea" — Phebe was a "servant" (the Greek diakonos) of the church at Cenchrea (the port of Corinth); the witnesses weigh whether this denotes a formal office (a deaconess, ministering to the women, the sick, the poor) or generally a faithful servant who ministered; the service; the deaconess or servant; the ministering woman; the office debated. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the faithful woman: Phebe, a faithful servant of the church, is honoured by Paul's commendation; the honour; the faithful woman commended; the service recognized; the woman's ministry valued; the honour given. The witnesses observe the bearer of the letter: Phebe, by the subscription, was the bearer of the epistle to Rome — entrusted with the precious letter; the trust; the bearer; the entrusted sister; the carrier of the epistle; the honour of the charge. The expositors note the prominence of women in the early church: the chapter (Phebe, Priscilla, Mary, and others) shows the prominence of women in the labour and service of the early church; the women's place; the prominence; the labouring women; the honour to the sisters; the women's ministry. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Phebe our sister, the servant of the church, and the honour to the faithful woman. The verse thus records the commendation of Phebe — teaching the honour given to a faithful woman who served the church, and the practice of letters of commendation.
2:
"That ye receive her in the Lord, as becometh saints, and that ye assist her in whatsoever business she hath need of you: for she hath been a succourer of many, and of myself also." The witnesses note the charge to receive and assist Phebe. Gill notes Paul recommends that they receive her in a kind and friendly manner — into their houses, their hearts' affections, their meetings and fellowship — as one in the Lord, and for his sake. The witnesses note Paul charging the church to receive Phebe in the Lord as becomes saints, and to assist her in her business, because she had been a succourer of many, and of Paul himself; the hospitable reception and practical help due to a fellow-saint, grounded in her own faithful succour of others — the mutual care of the body, the kindness returned to the kind; the reception of the saint. The witnesses note receive her in the Lord. The witnesses note a succourer of many.

The witnesses note the charge to receive and assist Phebe — the hospitable care due a fellow-saint, returned to one who succoured many.

So the witnesses make the verse the reception and assistance of Phebe. The lesson the expositors draw is the hospitable care due a fellow-saint, grounded in her own faithful succour of others. The witnesses note the reception: "receive her in the Lord, as becometh saints" — Gill notes Paul recommends a kind, friendly reception — into their houses, hearts, and fellowship, as one in the Lord; the reception; the welcome; the receiving in the Lord; the hospitable embrace; the becoming reception. The expositors observe the assistance: "assist her in whatsoever business she hath need of you" — they are to assist her in her business (her affairs, perhaps the church's business or her own); the assistance; the help; the practical aid; the business furthered; the support given. The witnesses press the ground — her succour of many: "for she hath been a succourer of many, and of myself also" — Phebe had been a succourer (helper, patroness) of many, and of Paul himself; the ground; the kindness returned; the succour of others; the helper helped; the kindness repaid. The expositors draw the lesson on the mutual care of the body: the body cares for its own — the kindness Phebe showed to many to be returned to her; the mutual care; the kindness repaid; the body's reciprocal love; the helper helped; the care returned. The witnesses observe the reception "as becometh saints": the reception is to be "as becometh saints" — in the manner befitting holy people, with love, kindness, and honour; the becoming manner; the saintly reception; the holy hospitality; the manner of the saints; the reception worthy of the calling. The expositors note Phebe's character as a patroness: Phebe was a patroness, a helper of many — a woman of means and generosity who succoured the saints and the apostle; the patroness; the generous helper; the succourer; the woman of substance; the benefactor of the church. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the receive her in the Lord, the succourer of many, and the care due a fellow-saint. The verse thus records the reception and assistance of Phebe — teaching the hospitable care due a fellow-saint, grounded in her own faithful succour of others.
3:
"Greet Priscilla and Aquila my helpers in Christ Jesus:" The witnesses note the greeting to Priscilla and Aquila. Gill notes Priscilla, the wife, is sometimes named before her husband Aquila (as here), sometimes after — for, as the Jews say, there is neither first nor last in Scripture; strict order is not always observed. The witnesses note Paul greeting Priscilla and Aquila, his helpers (fellow-workers) in Christ Jesus; the honoured couple who laboured with Paul in the gospel (tentmakers with whom he lodged at Corinth, who instructed Apollos at Ephesus), the wife often named first for her prominence in the work; the fellow-labourers honoured, the partnership of husband and wife in the service of Christ. The witnesses note Priscilla and Aquila. The witnesses note my helpers in Christ.

The witnesses note the greeting to Priscilla and Aquila, Paul's fellow-workers — the honoured couple who laboured with him in the gospel.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Priscilla and Aquila. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the fellow-labourers, the partnership of husband and wife in the service of Christ. The witnesses note the couple: "Priscilla and Aquila" — the honoured couple; Gill notes Priscilla (the wife) is sometimes named before her husband Aquila (as here), sometimes after; the couple; the husband and wife; the pair; the named couple. The expositors observe the helpers in Christ: "my helpers in Christ Jesus" — Priscilla and Aquila were Paul's helpers (fellow-workers, labourers) in Christ; the helpers; the fellow-workers; the labourers with Paul; the co-workers; the helpers in the gospel. The witnesses press their history with Paul: the couple were tentmakers with whom Paul lodged and worked at Corinth (Acts 18:2-3), who accompanied him to Ephesus and there instructed Apollos more perfectly (Acts 18:26); the history; the fellow-tentmakers; the instructors of Apollos; the companions; the labourers. The expositors draw the lesson on the partnership in service: husband and wife laboured together in the service of Christ — the partnership; the couple serving together; the wife and husband in the work; the joint labour; the marriage in the gospel's service. The witnesses observe Priscilla named first: Gill notes Priscilla is often named first — perhaps for her prominence in the work, her zeal, or her gifts (as in the instructing of Apollos); the wife named first; the prominence; the honour to the woman; the order; the wife's place in the labour. The expositors note the honour to the fellow-labourers: Paul honours the couple as his helpers — the fellow-labourers valued, named, greeted; the honour; the fellow-workers esteemed; the labourers honoured; the helpers named; the partnership valued. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Priscilla and Aquila, the helpers in Christ, and the partnership in service. The verse thus records the greeting to Priscilla and Aquila — teaching the honour to the fellow-labourers, the partnership of husband and wife in the service of Christ.
4:
"Who have for my life laid down their own necks: unto whom not only I give thanks, but also all the churches of the Gentiles." The witnesses note the couple's risking their lives for Paul. Gill notes "laid down their necks" means they exposed themselves to great danger to save the apostle's life — the allusion to execution by beheading; not literally done, but they risked their own lives for him. The witnesses note Paul recording that Priscilla and Aquila risked their lives for his, and that not only he but all the Gentile churches gave them thanks; the costly devotion of the couple, who hazarded their own lives for the apostle, and the gratitude of the whole Gentile church to them — for in saving Paul they served all the churches he laboured for; the sacrifice honoured. The witnesses note laid down their own necks. The witnesses note all the churches give thanks.

The witnesses note Priscilla and Aquila risking their lives for Paul — the costly devotion honoured by all the Gentile churches.

So the witnesses make the verse the couple's risking their lives. The lesson the expositors draw is the costly devotion of the couple and the gratitude of the whole church for those who served the apostle. The witnesses note the risk: "who have for my life laid down their own necks" — Gill notes "laid down their necks" means they exposed themselves to great danger to save Paul's life (the allusion to beheading); the risk; the danger braved; the lives hazarded; the necks laid down; the peril faced. The expositors observe the devotion: the couple risked their own lives to save Paul's — the costly devotion; the self-sacrifice; the love that hazards life; the devotion; the lives ventured; the sacrifice for the apostle. The witnesses press the gratitude of the churches: "unto whom not only I give thanks, but also all the churches of the Gentiles" — not only Paul but all the Gentile churches gave the couple thanks; the gratitude; the thanks of all; the churches' thanksgiving; the universal gratitude; the thanks of the Gentiles. The expositors draw the lesson on the service to all the churches: in saving Paul, the couple served all the churches he laboured for — therefore all gave thanks; the service to all; the churches indebted; the apostle's preservation serving all; the gratitude of the whole; the service multiplied. The witnesses observe the honour of the sacrifice: the couple's sacrifice is honoured — recorded, thanked, esteemed; the honour; the sacrifice valued; the devotion remembered; the risk honoured; the love recorded. The expositors note the example of devotion: Priscilla and Aquila are the example of devotion — risking their lives for the gospel's sake, for the apostle, for the churches; the example; the devoted couple; the model of sacrifice; the love that ventures all; the example of the faithful. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the laid down their own necks, the thanks of all the churches, and the costly devotion. The verse thus records the couple's risking their lives for Paul — teaching the costly devotion of the couple and the gratitude of the whole church.
5:
"Likewise greet the church that is in their house. Salute my well-beloved Epaenetus, who is the firstfruits of Achaia unto Christ." The witnesses note the house-church and Epaenetus the firstfruits. Chrysostom rebukes those who hasten over this part as superfluous (as they do the genealogy in the Gospel), thinking a catalogue of names yields no good — but the gold-founders are careful even of the little fragments; there is gold here too, in the honour to these saints. The witnesses note Paul greeting the church in Priscilla and Aquila's house, and Epaenetus, his well-beloved, the firstfruits of Achaia to Christ; the household church (the congregation meeting in a believer's home) and the honour to the first convert of a region — the firstfruits, the earnest of the harvest, dear to the apostle; the house-church and the firstfruits. The witnesses note the church in their house. The witnesses note the firstfruits of Achaia.

The witnesses note the house-church and Epaenetus the firstfruits of Achaia — the household congregation and the honour to the first convert.

So the witnesses make the verse the house-church and the firstfruits. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the household church and to the first convert of a region, and (per Chrysostom) the value of these names. The witnesses note the house-church: "greet the church that is in their house" — the church (congregation) met in Priscilla and Aquila's house; the household church; the congregation in the home; the house-church; the believers meeting in the home; the early gathering. The expositors observe the early house-churches: the early Christians met in private homes (before church buildings) — the household opened for worship; the house-churches; the homes for the gathering; the early meeting places; the open homes; the domestic congregations. The witnesses press Epaenetus the firstfruits: "Epaenetus, who is the firstfruits of Achaia unto Christ" — Epaenetus was the firstfruits of Achaia (the first convert of the region) to Christ; the firstfruits; the first convert; the earnest of the harvest; the firstling; the first won. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the first convert: the firstfruits (the first convert) is honoured — the earnest and pledge of the harvest to follow; the honour; the first convert dear; the firstfruits valued; the earnest of more; the first won esteemed. The witnesses observe Chrysostom's rebuke of those who skip the names: Chrysostom rebukes those who hasten over this part as superfluous (as the genealogies) — for the gold-founders are careful even of the fragments; there is gold here, in the honour to the saints; the rebuke; the value of the names; the gold in the list; the honour recorded; the names not superfluous. The expositors note the honour to the saints in the list: the catalogue of names honours the faithful saints — recording their labour, their love, their service; the honour; the saints remembered; the names valued; the faithful recorded; the gold of the list. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the church in their house, the firstfruits of Achaia, and the honour to the saints. The verse thus records the house-church and Epaenetus the firstfruits — teaching the honour to the household church and the first convert, and the value of these names.
6:
"Greet Mary, who bestowed much labour on us." The witnesses note the greeting to Mary, who laboured much. Gill notes some copies read "on you," and it seems likely the persons on whom this good woman bestowed so much labour, and to whom she was serviceable, were the saints at Rome where she lived, rather than the apostle and his companions. The witnesses note Paul greeting Mary, who bestowed much labour on the church; the honour to a woman who laboured much in the service of the saints — the diligent, abundant labour of a faithful sister, recorded and honoured by the apostle; the woman's labour valued, the much-labouring sister remembered; the labour honoured. The witnesses note Mary. The witnesses note much labour bestowed.

The witnesses note the greeting to Mary, who bestowed much labour — the honour to a faithful sister's diligent, abundant service.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Mary. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to a woman who laboured much in the service of the saints. The witnesses note the greeting: "Greet Mary" — Paul greets Mary; the greeting; the named sister; the salutation; the woman greeted; the saint named. The expositors observe the labour: "who bestowed much labour on us" — Mary bestowed much labour; Gill notes some copies read "on you," likely the saints at Rome where she lived; the much labour; the abundant service; the diligent toil; the labour bestowed; the work done. The witnesses press the much labour: Mary "bestowed much labour" — not a little, but much; the diligent, abundant service; the toil; the labour of love; the much work; the abundant labour. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the woman's labour: Mary's much labour is honoured — recorded, remembered, esteemed; the honour; the labour valued; the woman's service esteemed; the toil remembered; the labour honoured. The witnesses observe the object — the church: Mary's labour was bestowed on the church (the saints at Rome) — the service to the body; the object; the saints served; the church helped; the labour for the saints; the service to the body. The expositors note the prominence of women's labour: Mary, like Phebe and Priscilla, shows the prominence of women's labour in the early church; the women's labour; the prominence; the sisters' service; the women honoured; the labouring women. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Mary, the much labour bestowed, and the honour to the faithful sister. The verse thus records the greeting to Mary — teaching the honour to a woman who laboured much in the service of the saints.
7:
"Salute Andronicus and Junia, my kinsmen, and my fellow-prisoners, who are of note among the apostles, who also were in Christ before me." The witnesses note the greeting to Andronicus and Junia. Gill notes they were Paul's kinsmen according to the flesh (perhaps of the same tribe and family), fellow-prisoners (having suffered imprisonment for the gospel), of note among the apostles, and in Christ before him. The witnesses note Paul greeting Andronicus and Junia — his kinsmen, fellow-prisoners, of note among the apostles, and converted before him; the honoured pair, distinguished in the apostolic circle and early in the faith; the witnesses note the debates — whether Junia was a woman or a man (Junias), and whether they were "of note among the apostles" (apostles themselves) or well-known to the apostles; the honour to the early, suffering saints. The witnesses note fellow-prisoners. The witnesses note of note among the apostles.

The witnesses note Andronicus and Junia — kinsmen, fellow-prisoners, of note among the apostles, in Christ before Paul; the early, suffering saints honoured.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Andronicus and Junia. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the early, suffering, distinguished saints, with the notable debates on their identity and standing. The witnesses note the kinship and suffering: "my kinsmen, and my fellow-prisoners" — Gill notes they were Paul's kinsmen (according to the flesh, perhaps of the same tribe and family) and fellow-prisoners (having suffered imprisonment for the gospel); the kinsmen; the fellow-prisoners; the relatives; the suffering companions; the bonds shared. The expositors observe the standing: "who are of note among the apostles" — they were of note (distinguished, well-regarded) among the apostles; the standing; the distinction; the note; the regard; the honour in the apostolic circle. The witnesses present the debate on Junia's sex: the witnesses note the debate — whether "Junia" was a woman (the more natural reading of the name) or a man ("Junias," a contracted form); the early church (and the name's natural form) favoured a woman; the debate; Junia the woman or Junias the man; the disputed identity. The expositors present the debate on "of note among the apostles": the witnesses note the second debate — whether "of note among the apostles" means they were themselves apostles (in the wider sense, notable apostles), or were well-known to and esteemed by the apostles; the debate; apostles or well-known to apostles; the disputed standing; the two readings. The witnesses observe their early conversion: "who also were in Christ before me" — they were in Christ (converted) before Paul; the early conversion; the senior in the faith; the converted before Paul; the early believers; the seniority in Christ. The expositors note the honour to the early saints: Andronicus and Junia are honoured — kinsmen, fellow-sufferers, distinguished, early in the faith; the honour; the early saints esteemed; the suffering ones valued; the distinguished honoured; the faithful recorded. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the fellow-prisoners, the of note among the apostles, and the honour to the early, suffering saints. The verse thus records the greeting to Andronicus and Junia — teaching the honour to the early, suffering, distinguished saints, with the debates on their identity and standing.
8:
"Greet Amplias my beloved in the Lord." The witnesses note the greeting to Amplias, beloved in the Lord. Gill notes this was a Roman name (Ampliatus in some copies); the apostle had a singular affection for him, and that not on any external account — as natural relation, riches, or honour — but in the Lord. The witnesses note Paul greeting Amplias, his beloved in the Lord; the affection of the apostle for a fellow-believer, grounded not in worldly ties but in their common union with Christ — the spiritual love that binds the saints, the "in the Lord" the bond and the reason of the affection; the beloved in the Lord. The witnesses note Amplias. The witnesses note beloved in the Lord.

The witnesses note the greeting to Amplias, beloved in the Lord — the spiritual affection grounded not in worldly ties but in union with Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Amplias. The lesson the expositors draw is the spiritual affection of the apostle, grounded in the common union with Christ, not in worldly ties. The witnesses note the greeting: "Greet Amplias" — Paul greets Amplias; Gill notes a Roman name (Ampliatus); the greeting; the named believer; the salutation; the saint greeted. The expositors observe the affection: "my beloved in the Lord" — Amplias was Paul's beloved; the affection; the love; the dear believer; the beloved; the cherished saint. The witnesses press the ground — "in the Lord": Gill notes Paul's affection was not on any external account (natural relation, riches, honour) but "in the Lord"; the ground; the spiritual love; the union with Christ the bond; the love in the Lord; the bond not worldly. The expositors draw the lesson on the spiritual love of the saints: the love between the saints is grounded in their common union with Christ — not in worldly ties, but in the Lord; the spiritual love; the union the bond; the love in Christ; the saints bound in the Lord; the bond of grace. The witnesses observe the "in the Lord" the reason: the affection is "in the Lord" — both the sphere (within the union) and the reason (because of Christ) of the love; the in the Lord; the bond and the reason; the love because of Christ; the union the cause; the Lord the ground. The expositors note the affection for the obscure saint: Amplias may have been an obscure believer (perhaps a slave or freedman, the name common among such) — yet Paul loved him in the Lord; the love for the obscure; the affection regardless of station; the love that levels; the obscure saint beloved; the worth in Christ. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Amplias, the beloved in the Lord, and the spiritual love grounded in Christ. The verse thus records the greeting to Amplias — teaching the spiritual affection grounded in the common union with Christ, not in worldly ties.
9:
"Salute Urbane, our helper in Christ, and Stachys my beloved." The witnesses note the greeting to Urbane and Stachys. Gill notes Urbane was a Roman name; he is called "our helper in Christ" — a fellow-labourer; some conjecture he was one of the pastors of the church at Rome. The witnesses note Paul greeting Urbane, his (and the church's) helper in Christ, and Stachys, his beloved; the fellow-helper in the work and the beloved fellow-believer — the honour to those who help in the labour of the gospel and the affection for the beloved saints; the helper and the beloved both greeted, the labour and the love of the body. The witnesses note our helper in Christ. The witnesses note Stachys my beloved.

The witnesses note the greeting to Urbane the helper and Stachys the beloved — the honour to the fellow-worker and the affection for the saint.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Urbane and Stachys. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the fellow-helper in the work and the affection for the beloved saint. The witnesses note Urbane: "Salute Urbane, our helper in Christ" — Gill notes Urbane was a Roman name, called "our helper in Christ" (a fellow-labourer); some conjecture a pastor at Rome; the helper; the fellow-worker; the labourer; the helper in Christ; the co-worker. The expositors observe the "our helper": Urbane was "our helper" — not Paul's only, but the church's helper, the common fellow-worker; the our helper; the common labourer; the helper of all; the fellow-worker; the church's helper. The witnesses press Stachys the beloved: "and Stachys my beloved" — Stachys was Paul's beloved; the beloved; the affection; the dear saint; the cherished believer; the beloved fellow-believer. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the helper: Urbane, the fellow-helper in the work, is honoured — the labourer in the gospel esteemed; the honour; the helper valued; the fellow-worker esteemed; the labour honoured; the helper greeted. The witnesses observe the affection for the beloved: Stachys, the beloved, is affectionately greeted — the love for the saint; the affection; the beloved cherished; the love; the dear one greeted; the affection shown. The expositors note the labour and love of the body: the two greetings show the labour (Urbane the helper) and the love (Stachys the beloved) of the body — the work and the affection; the labour and the love; the helper and the beloved; the service and the affection; the body's work and bond. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the our helper in Christ, the Stachys my beloved, and the honour and affection of the body. The verse thus records the greeting to Urbane and Stachys — teaching the honour to the fellow-helper and the affection for the beloved saint.
10:
"Salute Apelles approved in Christ. Salute them which are of Aristobulus' household." The witnesses note the greeting to Apelles the approved and Aristobulus' household. Gill notes Origen wondered whether Apelles was the same as Apollos, but had he been intended Paul would have said more; the character "approved in Christ" is very considerable. The witnesses note Paul greeting Apelles, approved in Christ, and the believers of Aristobulus' household; the honour to a tried and approved believer (proved in the faith, tested and found genuine), and the notice of the saints in a household (the believing servants or members of a family) — the approved saint and the household believers; the tested faith and the household of faith. The witnesses note approved in Christ. The witnesses note Aristobulus' household.

The witnesses note the greeting to Apelles the approved and Aristobulus' household — the honour to a tried believer and the household saints.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Apelles and Aristobulus' household. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to a tried and approved believer and the notice of the saints in a household. The witnesses note Apelles: "Salute Apelles approved in Christ" — Gill notes Origen wondered whether Apelles was Apollos, but Paul would have said more; the character "approved in Christ" is considerable; the greeting; the named saint; the approved believer; the salutation. The expositors observe the "approved in Christ": Apelles was "approved in Christ" — tried, tested, and found genuine in the faith; the approval; the tried believer; the tested faith; the genuine saint; the proved Christian. The witnesses press the honour of approval: to be "approved in Christ" is a high honour — the faith tried and found true, the believer proved; the honour; the approval; the tested and approved; the genuine; the proved saint. The expositors draw the lesson on the tried faith: Apelles' faith was tried (perhaps by persecution, temptation, trial) and approved — the genuine, tested faith; the trial; the proving; the tried faith; the approval through trial; "the trial of your faith" (1 Peter 1:7). The witnesses observe Aristobulus' household: "them which are of Aristobulus' household" — the believers of Aristobulus' household (his servants or family members who believed); the household; the believing servants or family; the saints in the house; the household believers; the family of faith. The expositors note the household believers: the saints in Aristobulus' household — perhaps believing slaves or members — are greeted; the household saints; the believers in the family; the gospel reaching the household; the saints among the servants; the faith in the house. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the approved in Christ, the Aristobulus' household, and the honour to the tried believer and the household saints. The verse thus records the greeting to Apelles and Aristobulus' household — teaching the honour to a tried and approved believer and the saints in a household.
11:
"Salute Herodion my kinsman. Greet them that be of the household of Narcissus, which are in the Lord." The witnesses note the greeting to Herodion and the believers of Narcissus' household. Gill notes Herodion was Paul's kinsman (of the same nation, tribe, or family). Clarke notes probably another relative of the apostle. The witnesses note Paul greeting Herodion, his kinsman, and those of Narcissus' household who are in the Lord; the honour to a kinsman in the faith and the notice of the believers in a household — with the careful qualification "which are in the Lord" (only those of the household who were believers); the kinsman and the believing members of the house; the gospel among kindred and households. The witnesses note Herodion my kinsman. The witnesses note which are in the Lord.

The witnesses note the greeting to Herodion the kinsman and the believers of Narcissus' household — the gospel among kindred and households.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Herodion and Narcissus' household. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to a kinsman in the faith and the notice of the believing members of a household. The witnesses note Herodion: "Salute Herodion my kinsman" — Gill notes Herodion was Paul's kinsman (of the same nation, tribe, or family); Clarke, probably another relative; the kinsman; the relative; the kindred saint; the kinsman in the faith. The expositors observe the kinship in the faith: Herodion was both Paul's kinsman (by blood) and a fellow-believer — the natural kinship crowned by the spiritual; the kinship; the relative believing; the kindred in Christ; the natural and spiritual bond; the kinsman in the Lord. The witnesses press the household of Narcissus: "them that be of the household of Narcissus, which are in the Lord" — the believers of Narcissus' household; the household; the believing members; the saints in the house; the family of faith; the household believers. The expositors draw the lesson on the qualification "in the Lord": Gill notes the careful qualification — only those of Narcissus' household "which are in the Lord" (the believers, not the whole household, some of whom may not have believed); the qualification; the believers only; the in the Lord; the believing members; the distinction. The witnesses observe the gospel among households: the gospel reached households — some members believing (those "in the Lord"), some perhaps not; the household evangelized in part; the gospel in the house; the believing members; the family partly won; the gospel among the household. The expositors note the honour to the kindred and household saints: Herodion (the kinsman) and the believing members of Narcissus' household are honoured — the kindred and household saints greeted; the honour; the kindred valued; the household believers esteemed; the saints among the family; the faith honoured. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Herodion my kinsman, the which are in the Lord, and the gospel among kindred and households. The verse thus records the greeting to Herodion and Narcissus' household — teaching the honour to a kinsman in the faith and the believing members of a household.
12:
"Salute Tryphena and Tryphosa, who labour in the Lord. Salute the beloved Persis, which laboured much in the Lord." The witnesses note the greeting to the labouring women. Gill notes Tryphena and Tryphosa were women, said to be noble women of Iconium whom the apostle converted; their names are Greek. The witnesses note Paul greeting Tryphena and Tryphosa, who labour in the Lord, and the beloved Persis, who laboured much in the Lord; the honour to faithful women who laboured in the Lord's work — the present labour of two and the abundant past labour of another — the diligent service of the sisters in the gospel, valued and recorded; the labouring women honoured, the labour "in the Lord." The witnesses note labour in the Lord. The witnesses note laboured much.

The witnesses note the labouring women — Tryphena, Tryphosa, and Persis who laboured much in the Lord — the honour to faithful sisters' service.

So the witnesses make the verse the labouring women. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to faithful women who laboured in the Lord's work. The witnesses note the two: "Tryphena and Tryphosa, who labour in the Lord" — Gill notes they were women (perhaps noble women of Iconium whom Paul converted); they labour in the Lord; the two; the labouring women; the present labour; the sisters serving; the workers. The expositors observe the beloved Persis: "the beloved Persis, which laboured much in the Lord" — Persis, the beloved, laboured much; the beloved; the much labour; the abundant service; the dear sister; the labourer. The witnesses press the labour "in the Lord": the women laboured "in the Lord" — in his work, his cause, his service; the labour in the Lord; the work for Christ; the service in the gospel; the labour for the Lord; the work in his name. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the labouring women: the women who laboured in the Lord are honoured — recorded, valued, esteemed; the honour; the labour valued; the women's service esteemed; the sisters honoured; the labour recorded. The witnesses observe the degrees of labour: the two labour ("who labour"), Persis laboured much ("laboured much") — the present and the abundant past labour; the degrees; the present and the much; the labour and the much labour; the service measured; the abundant toil. The expositors note the prominence of women's labour: Tryphena, Tryphosa, and Persis, like Phebe, Priscilla, and Mary, show the prominence of women's labour in the early church; the women's labour; the prominence; the sisters' service; the labouring women; the honour to the women. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the labour in the Lord, the laboured much, and the honour to the faithful women. The verse thus records the greeting to the labouring women — teaching the honour to faithful women who laboured in the Lord's work.
13:
"Salute Rufus chosen in the Lord, and his mother and mine." The witnesses note the greeting to Rufus and his mother. Gill notes Rufus was a Roman name; some have thought this the same Rufus who was a son of Simon of Cyrene (who carried Christ's cross, Mark 15:21), which is not unlikely. The witnesses note Paul greeting Rufus, chosen in the Lord, and his mother — whom Paul calls "mine" also; the honour to a choice, eminent believer (chosen, or distinguished, in the Lord) and the tender notice of his mother, who had been a mother to Paul too (in kindness and care) — the choice saint and the motherly woman who had cared for the apostle; the chosen one and the spiritual mother. The witnesses note Rufus chosen in the Lord. The witnesses note his mother and mine.

The witnesses note the greeting to Rufus the chosen and his mother — the choice believer and the motherly woman who cared for Paul.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to Rufus and his mother. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to a choice believer and the tender notice of the motherly woman who had cared for the apostle. The witnesses note Rufus: "Salute Rufus chosen in the Lord" — Gill notes a Roman name; some think this the son of Simon of Cyrene (Mark 15:21); the greeting; the named saint; the chosen believer; the salutation. The expositors observe the "chosen in the Lord": Rufus was "chosen in the Lord" — the witnesses note the sense, either chosen (elect) in the Lord generally, or choice, eminent, distinguished in the Lord (a select, excellent believer); the chosen; the choice; the elect or eminent; the distinguished; the select saint. The witnesses press the mother: "and his mother and mine" — Paul greets Rufus' mother, whom he calls "mine" also; the mother; the motherly woman; the mother of Rufus; the spiritual mother; the kind woman. The expositors draw the lesson on the spiritual motherhood: Paul calls Rufus' mother "mine" — for she had been a mother to him, in kindness, care, and love (though not by birth); the spiritual motherhood; the motherly care; the mother to Paul; the kindness of a mother; the care that mothered the apostle. The witnesses observe the tenderness of the notice: the notice of Rufus' mother is tender — Paul remembering her motherly care, calling her his own mother; the tenderness; the gratitude; the motherly care remembered; the affection; the tender remembrance. The expositors note the possible link to Simon of Cyrene: if this is the son of Simon of Cyrene (who bore Christ's cross), the family of the cross-bearer became believers — a touching link; the possible link; the cross-bearer's family; the Simon of Cyrene connection; the family won; the touching history. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Rufus chosen in the Lord, his mother and mine, and the choice believer and motherly woman. The verse thus records the greeting to Rufus and his mother — teaching the honour to a choice believer and the tender notice of the motherly woman who cared for the apostle.
14:
"Salute Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermas, Patrobas, Hermes, and the brethren which are with them." The witnesses note the greeting to a group of brethren. Gill notes the first two names are Greek; these persons, with Herodion before, are noticed in the Roman martyrology as disciples of the apostle — Asyncritus said to be bishop of Hyrcania, Phlegon of Marathon, Hermas of Philippi, of the seventy disciples. The witnesses note Paul greeting Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermas, Patrobas, Hermes, and the brethren with them; the salutation of a group of believers (perhaps a household or a congregation that met together) and the brethren associated with them — the honour to the saints, named and grouped, and the notice of the brethren who gathered with them; the company of saints greeted. The witnesses note the named brethren. The witnesses note the brethren with them.

The witnesses note the greeting to a group of brethren and those with them — the honour to the saints, named and grouped, the company of believers.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to a group of brethren. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the saints, named and grouped, and the notice of the brethren who gathered with them. The witnesses note the names: "Asyncritus, Phlegon, Hermas, Patrobas, Hermes" — Gill notes the first two are Greek names; these (with Herodion) are noticed in the Roman martyrology as disciples of the apostle, said to be of the seventy; the names; the named brethren; the saints listed; the believers named; the company. The expositors observe the brethren with them: "and the brethren which are with them" — the brethren associated with these — perhaps a household, a congregation, or a group that met together; the brethren with them; the associated saints; the company; the gathered believers; the group. The witnesses press the grouping: these five (and the brethren with them) seem a group — perhaps a household church or a congregation that met together; the grouping; the company; the gathered saints; the congregation; the household or group. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the saints: each named saint is honoured — recorded, greeted, remembered; the honour; the saints valued; the names recorded; the believers esteemed; the faithful greeted. The witnesses observe the notice of the unnamed brethren: "the brethren which are with them" — even the unnamed brethren are noticed and greeted; the unnamed honoured; the brethren with them; the whole company; the unnamed remembered; the all greeted. The expositors note the fellowship of the saints: the grouping and the "brethren with them" show the fellowship of the saints — gathered together, associated, in communion; the fellowship; the communion; the gathered saints; the company of believers; the fellowship of the body. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the named brethren, the brethren with them, and the honour to the company of saints. The verse thus records the greeting to a group of brethren — teaching the honour to the saints, named and grouped, and the notice of the brethren who gathered with them.
15:
"Salute Philologus, and Julia, Nereus, and his sister, and Olympas, and all the saints which are with them." The witnesses note the greeting to another group of saints. Gill notes Philologus is a Greek name (a man's, meaning a lover of learning); Julia is a woman's name, Roman, probably his wife. The witnesses note Paul greeting Philologus, Julia, Nereus and his sister, Olympas, and all the saints with them; the salutation of another group of believers (perhaps families, with husband and wife, brother and sister) and all the saints associated with them — the honour to the named saints and the comprehensive greeting to all the saints gathered with them; the families and the company of saints greeted. The witnesses note the named saints. The witnesses note all the saints with them.

The witnesses note the greeting to another group of saints and all with them — the honour to the named and the comprehensive greeting to all the saints.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting to another group of saints. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the named saints and the comprehensive greeting to all the saints gathered with them. The witnesses note the names: "Philologus, and Julia, Nereus, and his sister, and Olympas" — Gill notes Philologus a Greek name (a lover of learning), Julia a Roman woman's name (probably his wife); the names; the named saints; the believers listed; the company; the saints named. The expositors observe the families: the grouping suggests families — Philologus and Julia (husband and wife), Nereus and his sister; the families; the husband and wife; the brother and sister; the kindred; the households. The witnesses press the comprehensive greeting: "and all the saints which are with them" — Paul greets all the saints with them — the whole company, named and unnamed; the comprehensive greeting; the all the saints; the whole company; the named and unnamed; the all included. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the named saints: each named saint is honoured — Philologus, Julia, Nereus, his sister, Olympas; the honour; the saints valued; the names recorded; the believers esteemed; the faithful greeted. The witnesses observe the all the saints: "all the saints which are with them" — even the unnamed saints are greeted; the all; the comprehensive; the unnamed included; the whole company; the all the saints honoured. The expositors note the fellowship and the comprehensiveness: the grouping (families) and the "all the saints with them" show the fellowship and the comprehensiveness of Paul's love — embracing all the saints; the fellowship; the comprehensiveness; the all embraced; the love for all; the whole body greeted. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the named saints, the all the saints with them, and the comprehensive greeting. The verse thus records the greeting to another group of saints — teaching the honour to the named saints and the comprehensive greeting to all the saints gathered with them.
16:
"Salute one another with an holy kiss. The churches of Christ salute you." The witnesses note the holy kiss and the salutation of the churches. Gill notes Christian salutation is a wishing all temporal, spiritual, and eternal happiness to one another, hearty and sincere; the "holy kiss" alludes to the custom of friends kissing at meeting or parting, in token of love and affection. The witnesses note Paul exhorting the saints to salute one another with a holy kiss, and conveying the greeting of the churches of Christ; the token of holy, sincere love among the saints (the kiss of charity, pure and reverent) and the mutual greeting of the churches — the fellowship and love within the congregation and between the churches; the holy kiss and the communion of the churches. The witnesses note the holy kiss. The witnesses note the churches salute you.

The witnesses note the holy kiss and the greeting of the churches — the token of holy love among the saints and the communion of the churches.

So the witnesses make the verse the holy kiss and the churches' greeting. The lesson the expositors draw is the token of holy love among the saints and the mutual communion of the churches. The witnesses note the holy kiss: "salute one another with an holy kiss" — Gill notes the "holy kiss" alludes to the custom of friends kissing at meeting or parting, in token of love; the saints to salute one another so; the holy kiss; the token of love; the kiss of charity; the greeting of affection. The expositors observe the holiness of the kiss: the kiss is "holy" — pure, reverent, chaste, free from all impropriety; the holiness; the pure kiss; the chaste token; the reverent greeting; the holy charity. The witnesses press the token of sincere love: the holy kiss is the token of holy, sincere love among the saints — the kiss of charity (1 Peter 5:14), the sign of hearty affection; the token; the sincere love; the affection; the charity; the love expressed. The expositors draw the lesson on the love within the congregation: the holy kiss expresses the love within the congregation — the saints loving one another sincerely; the love; the congregation's affection; the mutual love; the fellowship; the saints' charity. The witnesses observe the churches' greeting: "the churches of Christ salute you" — Paul conveys the greeting of the churches of Christ; the churches' salutation; the mutual greeting; the communion of the churches; the churches' love; the fellowship between churches. The expositors note the communion of the churches: the churches greeting one another shows the communion of the churches — the fellowship between congregations, the one body of Christ; the communion; the fellowship; the churches' bond; the one body; the catholic communion. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the holy kiss, the churches salute you, and the love within and between the churches. The verse thus records the holy kiss and the churches' greeting — teaching the token of holy love among the saints and the mutual communion of the churches.
17:
"Now I beseech you, brethren, mark them which cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned; and avoid them." The witnesses note the warning against the causers of division. Chrysostom notes how gently Paul exhorts — not as a counsellor but as a servant, with much respect, calling them brethren and supplicating them ("I beseech you, brethren"). The witnesses note Paul beseeching the brethren to mark (note, observe, be on guard against) those who cause divisions and offences contrary to the doctrine they had learned, and to avoid them; the warning against false teachers and troublers who divide the church and lay stumbling-blocks contrary to the apostolic doctrine — to be identified and shunned; the protection of the church from the dividers. The witnesses note mark them which cause divisions. The witnesses note avoid them.

The witnesses note the warning to mark and avoid those who cause divisions contrary to the doctrine — the protection of the church from dividers.

So the witnesses make the verse the warning against the causers of division. The lesson the expositors draw is the duty to identify and shun the false teachers who divide the church contrary to the apostolic doctrine. The witnesses note the beseeching: "I beseech you, brethren" — Chrysostom notes how gently Paul exhorts — not as a counsellor but as a servant, with respect, calling them brethren; the beseeching; the gentle entreaty; the tender appeal; the brethren addressed; the supplication. The expositors observe the marking: "mark them which cause divisions and offences" — the brethren are to mark (note, observe, be on guard against) those who cause divisions (splits, factions) and offences (stumbling-blocks, occasions of falling); the marking; the noting; the watching; the identifying; the being on guard. The witnesses press the standard — "contrary to the doctrine": the divisions and offences are "contrary to the doctrine which ye have learned" — against the apostolic teaching they had received; the standard; the doctrine; the apostolic teaching; the truth received; the rule of doctrine. The expositors draw the lesson on identifying the dividers: the dividers are to be identified by their teaching — contrary to the received doctrine — and their effect — causing divisions and offences; the identifying; the marks of the divider; the contrary doctrine; the dividing effect; the test. The witnesses observe the avoiding: "and avoid them" — the brethren are to avoid (shun, turn away from) the dividers; the avoiding; the shunning; the separation; the turning away; the no fellowship; the avoidance. The expositors note the protection of the church: the warning protects the church — the dividers marked and shunned, the church guarded from their influence; the protection; the church guarded; the dividers shunned; the flock protected; the truth defended. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the mark them which cause divisions, the avoid them, and the protection of the church. The verse thus records the warning against the causers of division — teaching the duty to identify and shun the false teachers who divide the church contrary to the apostolic doctrine.
18:
"For they that are such serve not our Lord Jesus Christ, but their own belly; and by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple." The witnesses note the character of the false teachers. Gill notes such serve not Christ — they preach not him but themselves, seek not his honour but their own things, worldly honour, riches, and grandeur, and seek to please men. The witnesses note Paul describing the dividers — they serve not Christ but their own belly (their appetites, gain, self-interest), and by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple; the self-serving motive and the deceptive method of the false teachers — serving themselves under the guise of religion, and beguiling the unwary by smooth, plausible speech; the marks of the deceiver. The witnesses note serve their own belly. The witnesses note deceive the simple.

The witnesses note the false teachers serving their own belly and deceiving the simple by fair speech — the self-serving motive and deceptive method.

So the witnesses make the verse the character of the false teachers. The lesson the expositors draw is the self-serving motive and the deceptive method of the false teachers. The witnesses note the not serving Christ: "they that are such serve not our Lord Jesus Christ" — Gill notes such serve not Christ — they preach not him but themselves, seek not his honour but their own; the not serving Christ; the self-seeking; the not Christ's servants; the false service; the not for Christ. The expositors observe the serving the belly: "but their own belly" — they serve their own belly — their appetites, gain, self-interest; the belly served; the self-interest; the gain; the appetite; the self-serving motive. The witnesses press the self-serving motive: the dividers serve themselves — their own profit, honour, comfort — under the guise of religion; the self-serving; the gain sought; the self-interest; the religion for profit; the belly the master. The expositors draw the lesson on the deceptive method: "by good words and fair speeches deceive the hearts of the simple" — they deceive by good words (plausible, smooth) and fair speeches (flattering, eloquent); the deceptive method; the smooth speech; the fair words; the flattery; the beguiling. The witnesses observe the victims — the simple: they deceive "the hearts of the simple" — the unwary, the unsuspecting, the undiscerning; the simple; the unwary; the undiscerning; the victims; the beguiled. The expositors note the marks of the deceiver: the marks of the false teacher are the self-serving motive (the belly) and the deceptive method (fair speech); the marks; the self-interest and the smooth words; the motive and the method; the deceiver identified; the false teacher's signs. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the serve their own belly, the deceive the simple, and the marks of the deceiver. The verse thus records the character of the false teachers — teaching the self-serving motive and the deceptive method of the dividers.
19:
"For your obedience is come abroad unto all men. I am glad therefore on your behalf: but yet I would have you wise unto that which is good, and simple concerning evil." The witnesses note the commendation of their obedience and the call to wisdom. Gill notes the fame of their obedience to the gospel was spread everywhere, commended among all the churches; this Paul mentions as a reason to beware of false teachers, lest they reproach their reputation. The witnesses note Paul rejoicing in the Romans' renowned obedience, yet desiring them to be wise unto good and simple (innocent) concerning evil; the commendation joined to the exhortation — glad of their good report, yet wishing them the wisdom to discern and embrace the good and the simplicity that keeps them innocent of evil; the wise and innocent saint. The witnesses note your obedience come abroad. The witnesses note wise unto good, simple concerning evil.

The witnesses note the commendation of their renowned obedience and the call to be wise unto good and innocent of evil — the wise, innocent saint.

So the witnesses make the verse the commendation and the call to wisdom. The lesson the expositors draw is the desire that the saints be wise unto good and innocent of evil. The witnesses note the commendation: "your obedience is come abroad unto all men" — Gill notes the fame of their obedience to the gospel was spread everywhere, commended among the churches; the commendation; the renowned obedience; the good report; the fame; the obedience known. The expositors observe the gladness: "I am glad therefore on your behalf" — Paul rejoices in their good report; the gladness; the joy; the rejoicing; the glad apostle; the joy in their obedience. The witnesses press the call to wisdom: "I would have you wise unto that which is good" — Paul desires them to be wise unto good — discerning, understanding, skilled in the good; the wise unto good; the discernment; the wisdom in good; the skill in the right; the understanding of good. The expositors draw the lesson on the simplicity concerning evil: "and simple concerning evil" — Paul desires them simple (innocent, harmless, unskilled) concerning evil — not versed in it, not entangled, kept innocent; the simple; the innocent; the harmless; the unskilled in evil; the kept pure. The witnesses observe the wise and innocent saint: the desire is for the wise-unto-good and simple-concerning-evil saint — discerning the good, innocent of the evil; the wise and innocent; the discerning and pure; the skilled in good and unskilled in evil; "wise as serpents, and harmless as doves" (Matthew 10:16); the balance. The expositors note the connection to the warning: the call to wisdom connects to the warning against the dividers (v.17-18) — the wise saint discerns the false teacher, the innocent saint is not beguiled; the connection; the wisdom against the deceiver; the discernment; the protection by wisdom; the innocence kept. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the obedience come abroad, the wise unto good, and the simple concerning evil. The verse thus records the commendation and the call to wisdom — teaching the desire that the saints be wise unto good and innocent of evil.
20:
"And the God of peace shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly. The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you. Amen." The witnesses note the promise of victory over Satan and the grace. Barnes notes "the God of peace" (who promotes peace) shall "bruise" (subdue, gain the victory over) Satan; the language refers to Genesis 3:15; it denotes Paul's confidence that they would overcome the arts of those sowing discord. The witnesses note Paul promising that the God of peace will shortly bruise Satan under their feet, and pronouncing the grace of the Lord Jesus upon them; the assurance of victory over Satan (the author of the divisions, echoing the first promise of Genesis 3:15, the serpent's head crushed), and the benediction of grace — the comfort and the blessing closing the warning; the God of peace conquering, the grace bestowed. The witnesses note bruise Satan under your feet. The witnesses note the grace of the Lord.

The witnesses note the God of peace bruising Satan under their feet, and the grace pronounced — the assured victory over Satan and the benediction.

So the witnesses make the verse the promise of victory and the grace. The lesson the expositors draw is the assurance of victory over Satan, echoing Genesis 3:15, and the benediction of grace. The witnesses note the God of peace: "the God of peace" — Barnes notes the God who promotes peace; the God of peace; the peace-giver; the God who reconciles; the author of peace; the God of concord. The expositors observe the bruising of Satan: "shall bruise Satan under your feet shortly" — Barnes notes "bruise" means subdue, gain the victory over; the language refers to Genesis 3:15; the bruising; the victory over Satan; the subduing; the crushing; the conquest. The witnesses press the echo of Genesis 3:15: the bruising of Satan under their feet echoes the first promise — the serpent's head crushed by the seed of the woman (Genesis 3:15); the echo; the first promise; the serpent's head; the seed's victory; the protevangelium fulfilled in the saints. The expositors draw the lesson on Satan the author of divisions: Satan is the author of the divisions (v.17-18) — and the God of peace will crush him under the saints' feet; the author of discord; the Satan behind the dividers; the victory over the divider; the discord crushed; the peace restored. The witnesses observe the shortly: "shortly" — the victory is near, soon; the shortly; the near victory; the soon conquest; the speedy triumph; the imminent victory. The expositors note the benediction: "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you" — Paul pronounces the grace of the Lord Jesus; the benediction; the grace; the blessing; the favour bestowed; the grace upon them. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the bruise Satan under your feet, the grace of the Lord, and the victory and the blessing. The verse thus records the promise of victory over Satan and the grace — teaching the assured victory over Satan, echoing Genesis 3:15, and the benediction of grace.
21:
"Timotheus my workfellow, and Lucius, and Jason, and Sosipater, my kinsmen, salute you." The witnesses note the greetings from Paul's companions. Gill notes now follow the salutations of the apostle's friends and companions; Timotheus (Timothy) is named first — the disciple Paul met at Derbe, whose mother was a Jewess and father a Greek. The witnesses note Paul conveying the greetings of his companions — Timothy his fellow-worker, and Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater his kinsmen; the salutations of the faithful companions who were with Paul as he wrote, sending their love to the Roman saints — the fellowship of the workers, Timothy the beloved son in the faith and the kinsmen who shared the labour; the companions' greeting. The witnesses note Timotheus my workfellow. The witnesses note my kinsmen.

The witnesses note the greetings from Paul's companions — Timothy and the kinsmen — the fellowship of the workers sending their love.

So the witnesses make the verse the greetings from Paul's companions. The lesson the expositors draw is the fellowship of the workers, the companions sending their love to the saints. The witnesses note Timothy: "Timotheus my workfellow" — Gill notes now follow the salutations of Paul's friends and companions; Timothy is named first — the disciple Paul met at Derbe (Acts 16:1); the workfellow; the fellow-worker; Timothy; the beloved son; the companion. The expositors observe the fellow-worker: Timothy was Paul's "workfellow" — fellow-labourer, beloved son in the faith, constant companion; the fellow-worker; the labourer; the son in the faith; the companion; the co-worker. The witnesses press the kinsmen: "Lucius, and Jason, and Sosipater, my kinsmen" — Lucius, Jason, and Sosipater, Paul's kinsmen (relatives, or fellow-Jews); the kinsmen; the relatives; the kindred; the fellow-Jews; the companions. The expositors draw the lesson on the fellowship of the workers: the companions — Timothy and the kinsmen — send their greetings; the fellowship; the workers together; the companions' love; the fellow-labourers; the company of Paul. The witnesses observe the sending of love: the companions send their salutations to the Roman saints — their love conveyed; the love sent; the greeting conveyed; the salutations; the affection sent; the love between distant saints. The expositors note the picture of the apostle's company: the verse pictures Paul's company as he wrote — surrounded by faithful companions, each sending his love; the picture; the company; the companions around Paul; the fellowship of the workers; the apostle not alone. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Timotheus my workfellow, the my kinsmen, and the fellowship of the workers. The verse thus records the greetings from Paul's companions — teaching the fellowship of the workers, the companions sending their love to the saints.
22:
"I Tertius, who wrote this epistle, salute you in the Lord." The witnesses note the greeting of Tertius, the scribe. Gill notes Tertius is a Latin name; he was certainly an amanuensis (a scribe), who wrote the epistle as Paul dictated it. The witnesses note Tertius, who wrote (penned) the epistle, sending his own greeting in the Lord; the notice of the scribe who took down the apostle's words, allowed to add his own salutation — the honour to the faithful amanuensis, and the glimpse of how the epistle was written (Paul dictating, Tertius writing); the scribe's greeting in the Lord. The witnesses note I Tertius who wrote this epistle. The witnesses note salute you in the Lord.

The witnesses note the greeting of Tertius the scribe who penned the epistle — the honour to the faithful amanuensis and the glimpse of its writing.

So the witnesses make the verse the greeting of Tertius the scribe. The lesson the expositors draw is the honour to the faithful amanuensis and the glimpse of how the epistle was written. The witnesses note the scribe: "I Tertius, who wrote this epistle" — Gill notes Tertius (a Latin name) was the amanuensis (scribe) who wrote the epistle as Paul dictated; the scribe; the amanuensis; the penman; the writer; Tertius. The expositors observe the writing of the epistle: Tertius "wrote" (penned) the epistle — Paul dictating, Tertius writing it down; the writing; the dictation; the penning; the amanuensis at work; the epistle taken down. The witnesses press the glimpse of the method: the verse gives a glimpse of how Paul's epistles were written — the apostle dictating, the scribe writing (as Paul often used an amanuensis, adding the closing in his own hand); the glimpse; the method; the dictation; the scribe and the apostle; the writing process. The expositors draw the lesson on the honour to the scribe: Tertius, the faithful scribe, is honoured — allowed to add his own greeting; the honour; the scribe valued; the amanuensis greeted; the faithful penman; the honour to the helper. The witnesses observe the greeting "in the Lord": "salute you in the Lord" — Tertius greets the saints in the Lord — his salutation grounded in their common union with Christ; the in the Lord; the greeting; the salutation; the love in the Lord; the bond of grace. The expositors note the inclusion of the humble helper: even the scribe, the humble helper, is included — sending his greeting, named in the inspired record; the inclusion; the humble helper; the scribe named; the helper honoured; the humble remembered. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the I Tertius who wrote this epistle, the salute you in the Lord, and the honour to the scribe. The verse thus records the greeting of Tertius the scribe — teaching the honour to the faithful amanuensis and the glimpse of how the epistle was written.
23:
"Gaius mine host, and of the whole church, saluteth you. Erastus the chamberlain of the city saluteth you, and Quartus a brother." The witnesses note the greetings of Gaius, Erastus, and Quartus. Gill notes Gaius (likely the one Paul baptized at Corinth) was the apostle's host and of the whole church — his hospitality and generosity well attested; some think him the Gaius to whom John wrote his third epistle. The witnesses note Paul conveying the greetings of Gaius his host (and host of the whole church), Erastus the city treasurer, and Quartus a brother; the honour to the hospitable Gaius who lodged the apostle and the church, the notice of Erastus a man of civil office among the believers, and the simple "a brother" for Quartus — the saints of various stations, host, official, and humble brother, all one in Christ; the company of saints. The witnesses note Gaius mine host. The witnesses note Erastus the chamberlain.

The witnesses note the greetings of Gaius the host, Erastus the treasurer, and Quartus a brother — saints of various stations, all one in Christ.

So the witnesses make the verse the greetings of Gaius, Erastus, and Quartus. The lesson the expositors draw is the company of saints of various stations — host, official, and humble brother — all one in Christ. The witnesses note Gaius: "Gaius mine host, and of the whole church" — Gill notes Gaius (likely the one baptized at Corinth, 1 Corinthians 1:14) was Paul's host and of the whole church; the host; the hospitable Gaius; the lodging of the apostle; the host of the church; the generous believer. The expositors observe the hospitality: Gaius was host to Paul and "of the whole church" — his home open to the apostle and the congregation; the hospitality; the open home; the host of the church; the generosity; the welcome. The witnesses press Erastus: "Erastus the chamberlain of the city" — Erastus, the chamberlain (treasurer) of the city — a man of civil office among the believers; the city treasurer; the official; the man of office; the civil servant; the believer in office. The expositors draw the lesson on the saints of various stations: the verse names saints of various stations — Gaius the host, Erastus the city treasurer, Quartus the humble brother; the various stations; the host, official, brother; the different ranks; the diversity; the saints of every kind. The witnesses observe Quartus "a brother": "Quartus a brother" — Quartus is simply "a brother" — no office, no distinction, just a brother in Christ; the brother; the simple title; the humble believer; the brother in Christ; the equality. The expositors note the unity in Christ: the saints of various stations — host, official, brother — are all one in Christ, all sending their love; the unity; the one in Christ; the various stations united; the equality in Christ; the one body. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks Gaius mine host, Erastus the chamberlain, and the saints of various stations one in Christ. The verse thus records the greetings of Gaius, Erastus, and Quartus — teaching the company of saints of various stations, all one in Christ.
24:
"The grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all. Amen." The witnesses note the repeated benediction of grace. Gill notes this verse is placed by the Syriac at the end of the chapter, and is wanting in some copies, being (except "all") the same as v.20; the reason for the repetition may be that the former was written by his amanuensis, and this with his own hand, as was Paul's custom in all his epistles. The witnesses note Paul repeating the benediction — the grace of the Lord Jesus Christ be with all the saints; the apostolic benediction of grace, perhaps written in Paul's own hand as his customary token, closing the greetings with the blessing of grace upon all; the grace pronounced, the Amen sealing it. The witnesses note the grace be with you all. The witnesses note the apostolic benediction.

The witnesses note the repeated benediction of grace upon all — the apostolic blessing, perhaps in Paul's own hand, closing the greetings.

So the witnesses make the verse the benediction of grace. The lesson the expositors draw is the apostolic benediction of grace, perhaps written in Paul's own hand, closing the greetings. The witnesses note the benediction: "the grace of our Lord Jesus Christ be with you all" — Paul pronounces the grace of the Lord Jesus upon all the saints; the benediction; the grace; the blessing; the favour; the grace bestowed. The expositors observe the repetition: Gill notes this verse (except "all") is the same as v.20, and is placed differently or wanting in some copies; the reason may be that the former was by the amanuensis, this in Paul's own hand; the repetition; the same blessing; the textual note; the closing benediction. The witnesses press the custom of Paul's own hand: Gill notes it was Paul's custom in all his epistles to write the closing benediction with his own hand (as a token of genuineness, 2 Thessalonians 3:17); the own hand; the token; the genuineness; the autograph; the apostolic seal. The expositors draw the lesson on the benediction of grace: the apostolic benediction is grace — the grace of the Lord Jesus, the sum of all blessing; the benediction; the grace; the blessing; the sum of good; the grace the great gift. The witnesses observe the "you all": "with you all" — the grace upon all the saints, without exception; the all; the comprehensive blessing; the grace for all; the whole church; the all included. The expositors note the Amen: "Amen" — the Amen seals the benediction, the affirmation of the blessing; the Amen; the seal; the affirmation; the so be it; the confirmation. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the grace be with you all, the apostolic benediction, and the grace pronounced upon all. The verse thus records the benediction of grace — teaching the apostolic blessing of grace, perhaps in Paul's own hand, closing the greetings.
25:
"Now to him that is of power to stablish you according to my gospel, and the preaching of Jesus Christ, according to the revelation of the mystery, which was kept secret since the world began," The witnesses note the opening of the doxology — God able to establish the saints. Gill notes God is described by his power, the particular instance being the establishing of his people — in the gospel and the profession of it, with grace in their hearts and in the exercise of it. Matthew Henry notes Paul solemnly closes his epistle with a magnificent ascription of glory to God. The witnesses note Paul opening the closing doxology — to him who is able to establish the saints according to the gospel and the preaching of Christ, according to the revelation of the mystery kept secret since the world began; the God of power who establishes his people in the gospel, the gospel itself a mystery long hidden, now revealed — the establishing power of God and the revealed mystery; the doxology begun. The witnesses note able to stablish you. The witnesses note the mystery kept secret.

The witnesses note the doxology opening — God able to establish the saints by the gospel, the revelation of the long-hidden mystery.

So the witnesses make the verse the opening of the doxology. The lesson the expositors draw is the establishing power of God by the gospel, which is the revelation of the mystery long kept secret. The witnesses note the power to establish: "to him that is of power to stablish you" — Gill notes God is described by his power, the instance being the establishing of his people; Matthew Henry notes the magnificent ascription of glory; the power; the establishing; the able God; the stablishing power; the God who confirms. The expositors observe the means — the gospel: "according to my gospel, and the preaching of Jesus Christ" — God establishes the saints according to the gospel (Paul's gospel) and the preaching of Christ; the means; the gospel; the preaching; the establishing by the word; the gospel the instrument. The witnesses press the revelation of the mystery: "according to the revelation of the mystery, which was kept secret since the world began" — the gospel is the revelation of the mystery (the hidden plan of salvation, especially the inclusion of the Gentiles) kept secret since the world began; the mystery; the revelation; the hidden plan; the secret revealed; the mystery made known. The expositors draw the lesson on the mystery now revealed: the mystery — long hidden in God's counsel, intimated in the prophets but not fully revealed — is now made known in the gospel; the mystery revealed; the hidden made known; the secret disclosed; the plan unveiled; the revelation in the gospel. The witnesses observe the establishing power of God: God is able to establish the saints — to confirm, strengthen, and settle them in the gospel; the establishing; the power to confirm; the saints settled; the God who stablishes; the strength imparted. The expositors note the gospel as the mystery: the gospel itself is "the mystery" — the revelation of God's hidden plan, especially the calling of the Gentiles into the one body; the gospel the mystery; the hidden plan; the Gentiles included; the mystery the gospel; the revealed secret. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the able to stablish you, the mystery kept secret, and the establishing power of God by the revealed gospel. The verse thus records the opening of the doxology — teaching the establishing power of God by the gospel, the revelation of the long-hidden mystery.
26:
"But now is made manifest, and by the scriptures of the prophets, according to the commandment of the everlasting God, made known to all nations for the obedience of faith:" The witnesses note the mystery now made manifest to all nations. Gill notes the mystery is now made manifest in these last days, in which God has spoken by his Son, by whom the doctrines of grace are most clearly revealed. Barnes notes it is now revealed, made known to all nations by the writings of the prophets and the gospel. The witnesses note Paul declaring that the mystery, once hidden, is now made manifest — by the scriptures of the prophets, according to God's commandment, made known to all nations for the obedience of faith; the unveiling of the mystery in the gospel age, witnessed by the prophets and proclaimed to all nations by God's command, that they might obey the faith; the mystery revealed and proclaimed for faith's obedience. The witnesses note now made manifest. The witnesses note made known to all nations.

The witnesses note the mystery now made manifest to all nations for the obedience of faith — the unveiling and proclamation of the hidden plan.

So the witnesses make the verse the mystery now made manifest. The lesson the expositors draw is the unveiling of the mystery in the gospel age, proclaimed to all nations by God's command for the obedience of faith. The witnesses note the manifestation: "but now is made manifest" — Gill notes the mystery is now made manifest in these last days, God having spoken by his Son; Barnes notes it is now revealed, made known; the manifestation; the revelation; the unveiling; the made manifest; the disclosed. The expositors observe the witness of the prophets: "by the scriptures of the prophets" — the mystery is witnessed by the scriptures of the prophets — intimated and foretold in the Old Testament, now fully revealed; the prophets' witness; the scriptures; the Old Testament foretelling; the prophetic testimony; the witness of the word. The witnesses press the command of God: "according to the commandment of the everlasting God" — the mystery is made known according to the commandment of the everlasting God — by his order, his authority; the command; the divine order; the everlasting God; the authority; the commandment. The expositors draw the lesson on the proclamation to all nations: "made known to all nations" — the mystery is made known to all nations — not the Jews only, but the Gentiles, all the world; the all nations; the universal proclamation; the Gentiles included; the world evangelized; the all nations reached. The witnesses observe the purpose — "the obedience of faith": "for the obedience of faith" — the mystery is made known that all nations might obey the faith — believe and submit to the gospel; the obedience of faith; the faith's obedience; the believing submission; the purpose; the faith and the obedience. The expositors note the everlasting God: the mystery is made known by the command of the "everlasting God" — the eternal God, whose plan, hidden from eternity, is now revealed in time; the everlasting God; the eternal; the God of the ages; the eternal plan; the everlasting purpose. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the now made manifest, the made known to all nations, and the mystery revealed for the obedience of faith. The verse thus records the mystery now made manifest to all nations — teaching the unveiling and proclamation of the hidden plan for the obedience of faith.
27:
"To God only wise, be glory through Jesus Christ for ever. Amen." The witnesses note the ascription of glory to the only wise God. Gill notes this may be understood of God the Father, the only wise God — originally, essentially, and infinitely wise (not excluding the Son and Spirit); whose wisdom appears in creation, providence, redemption, and the whole scheme of the gospel; and the glory of all is to be given to him through Christ as Mediator. The witnesses note Paul closing the epistle with the ascription of glory to God only wise, through Jesus Christ, for ever; the doxology that crowns the epistle — the glory ascribed to the all-wise God, whose wisdom is displayed in the whole plan of salvation, the glory rendered through Christ the Mediator, world without end; the only wise God glorified for ever. The witnesses note to God only wise. The witnesses note glory through Jesus Christ.

The witnesses note the ascription of glory to the only wise God through Christ for ever — the doxology crowning the epistle.

So the witnesses make the verse the ascription of glory to the only wise God. The lesson the expositors draw is the glory rendered to the all-wise God, whose wisdom is displayed in the plan of salvation, through Christ the Mediator. The witnesses note the only wise God: "to God only wise" — Gill notes this is God (the Father, not excluding the Son and Spirit), the only wise — originally, essentially, infinitely wise; the only wise; the all-wise; the wisdom of God; the wise God; the source of wisdom. The expositors observe the display of wisdom: God's wisdom appears in creation, providence, redemption, and the whole scheme of the gospel — the wisdom displayed; the manifold wisdom (Ephesians 3:10); the wisdom in salvation; the plan of grace; the wisdom shown. The witnesses press the glory: "be glory" — to God be glory — the praise, the honour, the ascription; the glory; the praise; the honour ascribed; the doxology; the glory rendered. The expositors draw the lesson on the glory through Christ: "through Jesus Christ" — the glory is rendered through Jesus Christ the Mediator — for all access to God and all praise to him is through Christ; the through Christ; the Mediator; the access by Christ; the praise through him; the glory mediated. The witnesses observe the "for ever": "for ever" — the glory is to be rendered for ever, world without end, eternally; the for ever; the eternal glory; the everlasting praise; the world without end; the endless doxology. The expositors note the crowning of the epistle: this doxology crowns the epistle — the glory ascribed to the only wise God, whose wisdom the whole epistle (the plan of justification, sanctification, the calling of Jew and Gentile) has displayed; the crown; the doxology; the epistle closed; the glory to God; the fitting close. The witnesses observe the Amen: "Amen" — the Amen seals the doxology, the affirmation of the praise; the Amen; the seal; the affirmation; the so be it; the close. The witnesses draw the application: the believer marks the to God only wise, the glory through Jesus Christ, and the only wise God glorified for ever. The verse thus records the ascription of glory to the only wise God — teaching the glory rendered to the all-wise God, whose wisdom is displayed in salvation, through Christ the Mediator, for ever.
